Governance has its roots in colonialism and assumes a political hierarchy that has historically been used to assert power/control over marginalized groups and indigenous populations.

The obsession with "governance" in Web3 and DWeb inhibits its own progress.

@browningphilip1 This reflection is based on how I see "governance" being understood and applied in a general context.

I'm not saying that governance can't result in positive outcomes, but that it has linguistic origins that assign power/control to the hands of a governor, implying hierarchy.
@omniharmonic Councils > governors

@robpertray Quite sad, really. 

Even "the left" has turned its back on the labor class.
RT @buchmanster: @todd_x_y Let’s just put the grandmas in charge and call it a day

@BraveIowan @browningphilip1 I think "control" or "to steer" is the frame of reference that I hope to bring into question. 

Governance is used as blanket terminology where decision-making and deliberation are needed, but (I believe) it is a loaded term that comes with baggage that needs to be unpacked.

@CfGS_Ed @browningphilip1 I don't disagree. 

My concern is that governance, implying hierarchy, will likely recreate patterns of dominance that are in contention with the goals of those who are employing it.

@RohitMalekar This is getting closer to the spirit of the original post.

Is "governance" the right terminology to use when we're trying to create spaces for decision-making and deliberation that take into account a wide spectrum of voices - outside of those who are "in control"
@gregory_landua Yes.
@0xdididoitright I'll let you know when I find out!

@MattPirkowski This resonates. 

To me, governance is "pointy" - if it were a shape, it would be a triangle. In a world full of triangles, circles pay a premium, but ultimately it's a balance that needs to be achieved. Neither should dominate.
@JTremback If it doesn't have "authorities" - I wouldn't call it governance.
If the word "governance" was a shape, what would it be?
@jeffjstephens @MattPirkowski I frequently sit in "circles" of dialogue with others. In these circles, all voices are welcome.

But, an overabundance of hierarchy - which we sometimes see and feel in daily life - can be a problem.

Governance, to me, appears as a highly refined system of law and policy in the creation of nation-states and the corporate form. 

These entities are held together through intangible agreements and contracts, but they have a very real impact on the physical world.

@JacqR5 I've been taking the perspective of DAO tooling as infrastructure. 

It's like water and HVAC in a house, very helpful when you need it, but hidden in the framework, and not the point of the group itself.

And when I say refined, I mean they are based on hundreds and thousands of years of legal precedent and informed by social and political norms.

Could it be too bold to make the claim that statecraft is the father of systems theory?
@Richard_Flyer @jeffjstephens @MattPirkowski Absolutely!

I see this being a challenge of legal engineering and see trusts as a potential custodial structure for these types of agreements.

What if post offices became bioregional coordination and intelligence hubs? 

They often define the geographic limits of civilization, serve as hubs in a global communications network, and exist at the last mile between humanity and the wild.
@Happyholistichs I want to help make this happen - by (nearly) any means possible.

According to USPS, the United States Postal Service has 31,247 retail locations. 

We could put a @H_O_L_O_ node in each for less than $20M total in hardware.

All we need is a civil "community board" app.
As a daily and essential public service, they also have the most significant need for weather and climate data.

The recent advancements in AI make me uncomfortable…

I think “Artificial Intelligence” is a misnomer and does a disservice to the fundamental technology. 

We train LMMs on collections of information that are derived from human knowledge, a byproduct of our own intelligence.

If you trained a model on the entirety of Shakespeare and it generates something *novel but coherent* with the Shakespearean body of work, it’s not technically *artificial* but actually *informed by* the pattern language of Shakespeare.

By calling it “artificial” - we seem to be devaluing and disassociating the body of knowledge and all of its source points, people, who produced the material on which the model was trained. 

If anything, the technology that’s been created is more of a “mirror intelligence”

Presented in this way, the technology itself could be perceived as a type of knowledge or intelligence “commons” or generative public good - which has a markedly different “spirit” than what we’re currently experiencing in mainstream “AI” discourse.

Names matter.

AI (as a type of “brand name” of an umbrella of technologies) will suffer from this disassociation of AI outputs (generated content) and AI inputs (training data) 

AI is a collective intelligence technology, inseparable from the connections that *constitute* the model.

This disassociation has fairly significant economic and power implications too, just look at GitHub copilot and the licensing structures of the code bases it was trained on, or Dall•e and the various forms media and art that it was “fed”

The corporate appropriation of commons property and works of art is a topic for another time, but it seems to be intimately related to how we collectively think about and discuss “AI” and its underlying enablers.

In short, the name “Artificial Intelligence” seems to frame and project a false separation, directly informing the “storytelling” of what otherwise is a potentially civilization-altering technology. 

As a technical innovation, the accuracy of its name should matter.

Preamble

In recognition of our shared responsibility to sustain and nurture the Earth's diverse and interconnected bioregions, we hereby proclaim this charter to guide our journey towards a harmonious, resilient, and thriving global community.

The Bioregional Commons shall be founded on the principles of social and environmental justice, grounded in scientific understanding, informed by the wisdom of the past, and driven by the collective pursuit of a prosperous and equitable future for all.
Article 2 - Local Knowledge and Wisdom

Each bioregion's unique cultures, traditions, and knowledge systems shall be respected and integrated into decision-making processes, with emphasis on preserving indigenous rights, honoring traditional ecological knowledge, and celebrating the rich tapestry of human experience.

Article 3 - Ecological Integrity

The integrity, resilience, and biodiversity of all bioregions shall be upheld through the responsible management of natural resources, guided by evidence-based principles and a deep understanding of ecological interdependence.

Article 4 - Regenerative Pracitices

The Bioregional Commons shall embrace regenerative practices that restore, replenish, and sustain ecosystems, ensuring the long-term wellbeing of all inhabitants, including future generations.

Article 5 - Social Equity and Inclusion

The rights, dignity, and wellbeing of every human being shall be honored and safeguarded within the Bioregional Commons, fostering social equity, inclusion, and empowerment, and ensuring access to essential resources for all.

Article 6  - Participatory Governance

Decision-making processes shall be transparent, accountable, and participatory, engaging diverse stakeholders, including local communities, in the planning, management, and evaluation of the Bioregional Commons.

Article 7 - Adaptive Learning

The Bioregional Commons shall embody a spirit of adaptive learning, incorporating new knowledge and innovations, and fostering ongoing reflection and improvement in response to evolving challenges and opportunities.

Article 8 - Global Solidarity

Recognizing the interconnectedness of all bioregions and the shared challenges of climate change and ecological degradation, the Bioregional Commons shall promote global solidarity, collaborative action, and mutual support.

Article 9 - Education and Awareness

Education and awareness-raising efforts shall be championed to nurture a deeper understanding of the Bioregional Commons, fostering a sense of belonging, responsibility, and stewardship among all generations.

Article 10 - Celebration and Renewal

The Bioregional Commons shall be a living testament to the beauty, wonder, and diversity of our planet, inspiring collective celebration, renewal, and a commitment to the flourishing of all life

Closing Statement

May this Charter of the Bioregional Commons serve as a beacon of hope, uniting us in our shared pursuit of a more just, sustainable, and vibrant world for all inhabitants, present and future.

Trusts, uses, and endowments (as a legal structure protecting the sharing of benefits) predate modern corporate law (with limited liability and equity instruments) by a good millennia or two.  

This fact brings me great peace.

1/ Trusts originated in 12th century English law, when knights needed to manage their estates during the Crusades. But the idea of managing property for the benefit of others has roots in many earlier cultures.

2/ Roman law included "fideicommissum," a testamentary practice similar to trusts, and "usufruct," which allowed use of another's property. Greek law had a similar concept, with guardianship arrangements for managing property for minors or others incapable of doing so themselves.

3/ In Islamic law, there was the "waqf," a charitable endowment much like a trust, intended for the benefit of the community. Ancient Indian law also had "Dharmadayada," an endowment for religious or charitable purposes.

4/ During China's Tang Dynasty, a system known as "yingshe" allowed an owner to transfer property while retaining the right to use it for life, similar to a life estate or revocable trust. The principle of separating legal and beneficial ownership is universal and longstandi

5/ The modern corporation began in the Middle Ages in Europe. However, shared liability business ventures are older. Ancient Rome had "societas publicanorum," public contractors sharing profits and losses.

6/ In the ancient Maurya Empire in India, there were large, state-owned companies. Similarly, during the 7th to 13th centuries in China, there were family-owned businesses known as "hongs" that were involved in international trade.

7/ The idea of shared business ventures is ancient, but the modern corporation—with its features of limited liability, perpetual succession, and transferable shares—is a relatively recent development.

8/ The concept of equity and stock, i.e., transferable shares of ownership, came about in late Middle Ages Europe. This was a key innovation that enabled the rise of large joint-stock companies like the Dutch East India Company.

9/ Earlier societies had shared ownership business structures, but without transferable shares like modern equity. The introduction of this concept significantly spurred the growth of complex business organizations.

10/ Trusts and corporations serve different roles and carry distinct risks. Trusts manage assets for the benefit of others, while corporations are business entities designed to generate profits for shareholders.
11/ Trusts can mitigate risk by diversifying assets and protecting them from creditors. They're also useful for estate planning, reducing tax burdens, and supporting charitable causes.

12/ However, trusts can be complex to set up and manage. They require a trustee, which can lead to conflicts of interest or mismanagement if the trustee does not act in the best interests of the beneficiaries.
13/ Corporations, on the other hand, enable businesses to raise capital and limit shareholders' liability. This encourages investment and fosters economic growth.

14/ Corporations, though, come with risks such as potential for mismanagement, conflicts of interest among stakeholders, and market volatility. They can also face significant regulatory scrutiny and complex tax obligations.

15/ In an economy dominated by corporations, trusts and similar benevolent entities are crucial. They ensure wealth can be managed and protected for specific purposes, outside the profit-driven corporate sphere.

16/ Trusts support philanthropy, manage wealth across generations, and provide a framework for unique situations where direct ownership isn't optimal. They add a layer of flexibility and protection in our economic system.

17/ While corporations drive much of our economy, trusts play an essential role in managing and preserving wealth, promoting charitable causes, and ensuring financial stability for beneficiaries. They form an important counterbalance in our diverse economic landscape.

Been thinking about this question since you asked it...

You're spot on and right at the heart of the matter. We need to think about value in more ways than just money. Traditional banks and financial systems don't appreciate this. 

For example, in housing, there are other means (like co-ops and land trusts) where people work together to own homes without emphasis on maximizing margins/profit - but emphasize value creation for a broad base of individuals - over financial gain solely for shareholders. This was the promise of DAOs.

This kind of co-op/trust-based model can be used in other industries/sectors, not just housing. The next system will recognize a more holistic spectrum of value creation, not just financial profits. 

Regarding your last point, I think there will definitely be some catch-up to do once we get better at qualifying other forms of value.

WORKING TITLE: *Cooperatives as Organizational Operating Systems*

or for you more academically minded folks...

*Corporate Bylaws as Code: Organizational Systems Design and…

I'm a [information] technologist, so if you'd entertain me for a few minutes, it's time for a story...

WORKING TITLE: *Cooperatives as Organizational Operating Systems*

or for you more academically minded folks...

*Corporate Bylaws as Code: Organizational Systems Design and Economic Outcomes*

Let's begin.

Part 1: The Two Organizational Operating Systems

Have you ever wondered how an Operating System (OS) is similar to the foundation of a company? Let's dive into this intriguing analogy!

An OS, like Windows or MacOS, is the backbone of your computer. It manages hardware, software, and resources - essentially setting the rules

Take @Microsoft's @Windows: It’s like a shareholder corporation. Shareholders invest and receive profits but don't manage daily ops. Windows serves many users, but they don’t control its core functions.

On the flip side, @Apple MacOS mirrors a cooperative. It's more user-centric and democratic, focusing on member (or user) needs and participation, much like a cooperative’s emphasis on member involvement.

Diving deeper into 'Cooperatives as Organizational Operating Systems': The structure of an organization, much like the code in an OS, shapes its functionality and impact on its users - in this case, the stakeholders - or players in the economy.

In shareholder corporations, bylaws are like proprietary code, focusing on efficiency and return on investment. Shareholders, akin to license holders, benefit from the profits, akin to software updates and enhancements.

Cooperatives operate like open-source systems, where the 'code' (bylaws) is democratically crafted and amended by its users (members). This model fosters community-driven development and equitable resource distribution.

The 'corporate code' in shareholder corporations often prioritizes financial growth, leading to significant economic returns for shareholders but also potentially widening economic disparities.

Conversely, the cooperative model, with its member-centric 'code', often recirculates profits among its members and into community projects, promoting more balanced economic growth.

Thus, the 'organizational OS' - whether a cooperative or a corporation - doesn’t just govern internal operations but also impacts the broader economic landscape, from wealth creation to social equity.

Understanding these organizational systems as 'operating systems' or 'codebases' offers us a unique lens to view their roles in our economy, highlighting the diverse effects they have from wealth generation to community empowerment.

Understanding these nuances helps us appreciate the diverse roles organizations play in our economy, from wealth generation to social empowerment. Each model offers unique advantages and challenges in our interconnected economic landscape.

Part 2: The Role of Operating Systems in Economic Outcomes

To summarize, just as an operating system governs a computer's functionality, the 'organizational code' - be it in cooperatives or corporations - dictates how an organization operates and interacts with its economic environment.

Think of the U.S. economy as a vast network of computers, each running different versions of 'organizational operating systems'. These systems are programmed with distinct codes (bylaws and governance structures) that profoundly affect their operations.

Now, imagine if we could 'reprogram' or tweak this code. How would altering the balance between cooperative-style and corporate-style governance impact overall economic outcomes?

What if more businesses operated on cooperative principles, prioritizing democratic decision-making and equitable distribution? Would this lead to a more balanced and resilient economy?

Conversely, what if corporate models with a focus on efficiency and shareholder returns became even more dominant? How would this shape wealth distribution, innovation, and economic growth?

This thought experiment highlights the significant influence of organizational structures on economic landscapes. The 'code' behind each organization not only drives its success but also shapes societal wealth and well-being.

Understanding these dynamics is crucial as we navigate an ever-evolving economic terrain, through valleys and peaks. It's not just about the businesses we create; it's about the underlying 'operating systems' we choose to implement and how they shape our collective future.

Part 3: Wait, so what about Finland now?

Looking at Finland, a country with a strong cooperative economy, we see a model where economic stability and community welfare go hand in hand. Cooperatives play a key role in various sectors, supporting both growth and social equality.

In Finland, cooperatives are not just small, niche players. They are major contributors to the economy, involved in everything from banking to retail, demonstrating that cooperative models can scale successfully.

For the U.S., there's much to learn from Finland's cooperative approach. Embracing more cooperative principles could lead to a more resilient economy, where success is measured not just in profits, but in how well it serves the community.

As we envision the future of our economy, let’s consider the diverse possibilities of organizational structures. Perhaps a hybrid model, blending the efficiency of corporations with the inclusivity of cooperatives, could pave the way.

To end, the 'organizational operating systems' we choose greatly influence our economic landscape. Exploring and learning from models like Finland's can inspire new ways to structure our economy for a more equitable and prosperous future for all.

detailed: my lens and (opinionated) critique here is of the [commercial] corporate form, which most organizations fall into (at least here in the…

short answer: yes, as corporate governance is emergent from public institutions, this should be recognized in corporate taxation. 

detailed: my lens and (opinionated) critique here is of the [commercial] corporate form, which most organizations fall into (at least here in the U.S.), so I'll expand on what I'm implying:

specific shareholders in this case: "owners" and controllers of companies, by power: executives, board, management

broad stakeholders in this case: customers, non-shareholder workers, partners/vendors, the sector/industry itself, and other living (non-human) beings (flora/fauna foundational to natural environments)

Your question touched on responsibility, which is subjective, but I understand that the corporate form is a created artifact of a public regulatory framework, so there should be some responsibility of the corporate entity on behalf of the public body that enables it. 

I see public institutions as responsible for people and place - the collective body and the collective home. At least conceptually, nations and states should be responsible for the welfare and governance of the people and the land, for the benefit of both. Law and regulation are the logical tools for the fulfillment of these principal roles, including corporate law, which is designed to control the market of economic outputs. 

In the greater global economy, corporate entities no longer recognize themselves as being emergent from public legal frameworks and now working intra-jurisdictionally in an attempt to minimize tax liability, resulting in a failure to ensure broad value return.

I like this!

Through this lens, I could argue that the corporate form is emergent from [public] institutional culture. 

I’ve come to see organizational forms as being institutionally codified structures of collective expression, some models (co-ops or trust companies) being more conducive to expression than others (shareholder corporations). 

It’s a wide spectrum and co-ops aren’t perfect, but I believe they’re potentially capable of recognizing/appreciating the sacred/invaluable dimensions of life and work within the context of complex systems.

Thoughts on the @Cisco patent filing integrating @holochain - as a long-time follower, an active Holo host, and a formerly certified (3x) Cisco associate.

Although my certification was in network structure and security, this patent news caught my eye. My initial insight was that the filing uses the pattern language of hREA applied to a new form of distributed data storage. 

Here, Cisco is using telecommunications infrastructure, which they term "fog computing" - which I interpret as an adaptation of the common "cloud computing."

Some basics on the patent filing:

This patent - introducing fog computing - describes techniques to allow different entities in a 5G network, like service providers and software companies, to securely share information with each other using Holochain technology.

Holochain allows distributed sharing of information between entities without needing a centralized database. Each entity maintains its own transaction records but can publish authenticated transactions to a shared database that all entities can access.

1. The entities conducting transactions using "fog computing" could be seen as economic agents, similar to hREA's agents that track resource flows.
2. The network slice information being captured in transactions mirrors how hREA tracks information flows between agents.
3. Capturing provenance and history of usage data is analogous to hREA's principles of provenance tracking.

To better understand the patent, it's important to consider the hREA framework and why it's unique in the Holochain ecosystem.

If you can get through the first few paragraphs, this introduction to the @Holochain Resource-Event-Agent (hREA) language is an inspiring example of how the #Holo network might serve as a trusted data layer for locally coordinated economic exchange.

https://t.co/nAJXjaOHLV

Article TL;DR

The examples provided here are in food and textile networks, but the framework can essentially be applied to any sector or industry. This tech could enable organizations to integrate data exchange mechanisms for better supply chain coordination - between suppliers of a resource and buyers of resources.

As a host, the hREA language is one of the more intriguing applications I’ve seen emerge in the 5 years I’ve been following the project.

While it’ll still be a while before hREA might be leveraged in more “mainstream” commercial tools, the Cisco patent is somewhat encouraging. However, I could see early prototypes emerging to support smaller, local-level projects. This could be interesting for supporting farmers or community-supported agriculture (#CSA) groups.

What might it look like for smaller farms and family operations to be able to sidestep the dominant market players in agriculture, potentially selling direct-to-consumer through new tech?

Following in the example case - what role might #HOT play as the base network currency between different land stewards and agricultural producers across bioregions - the foundation of local ecology, habitat, food systems, and fiber sheds?

If you have the time to read it, check out the article and see if you can intuit what might be emerging here - it’s exciting stuff!

Thoughts on the @Cisco patent filing integrating @holochain - as a long-time follower, an active Holo host, and a formerly certified (3x) Cisco associate.

Although my certification was in network structure and security, this patent news caught my eye. My initial insight was that the filing uses the pattern language of hREA applied to a new form of distributed data storage. 

Here, Cisco is using telecommunications infrastructure, which they term "fog computing" - which I interpret as an adaptation of the common "cloud computing."

Some basics on the patent filing:

This patent - introducing fog computing - describes techniques to allow different entities in a 5G network, like service providers and software companies, to securely share information with each other using Holochain technology.

What information are they sharing? Let's look at how the patent applies to Network Slicing in telecommunications.

Network Slicing 101
Imagine a 5G network as a multi-lane highway. Just as different lanes can be designated for different types of vehicles (like cars, buses, or bikes), in a 5G network, "network slicing" allows the creation of multiple virtual networks (or 'slices') over the same physical network infrastructure. Each slice can be tailored to meet specific requirements of different applications or services. For example, one slice could be optimized for high-speed data for streaming videos, while another could be configured for low-latency communication needed in autonomous vehicles.

Resource Orchestration Using Holochain
Think of "resource orchestration" as the traffic management system for this highway. It ensures that each vehicle (or data packet) is on the right lane (or network slice) and that traffic flows smoothly without congestion. In the context of this patent, Holochain acts like a highly advanced and decentralized traffic management system. It uses blockchain-like technology to securely and efficiently manage how these network slices are allocated and used.

STEP 1 - When a service (like a streaming service or an IoT device) needs a specific type of network slice, it sends a request (a Holochain transaction) specifying its needs.
STEP 2 - This request is received by the 5G network provider, who reviews it and, if agreed, co-signs the transaction.
 STEP 3 - This mutual agreement is then recorded in a secure and transparent manner, thanks to Holochain's technology.
STEP 4 - Finally, the agreed-upon network slice is allocated to the service, ensuring it gets the network resources it needs.

In this example, Holochain allows distributed sharing of network information between entities without needing a centralized database. 

Cisco Patent and hREA Pattern Language

1. The entities conducting transactions using "fog computing" could be seen as economic agents, similar to hREA's agents that track resource flows.
2. The network slice information being captured in transactions mirrors how hREA tracks information flows between agents.
3. Capturing provenance and history of usage data is analogous to hREA's principles of provenance tracking.

To better understand the patent, it's important to consider the hREA framework and why it's unique in the Holochain ecosystem.

If you can get through the first few paragraphs, this introduction to the @Holochain Resource-Event-Agent (hREA) language is an inspiring example of how the #Holo network might serve as a trusted data layer for locally coordinated economic exchange.

https://t.co/nAJXjaOHLV

Article TL;DR

The examples provided here are in food and textile networks, but the framework can essentially be applied to any sector or industry. This tech could enable organizations to integrate data exchange mechanisms for better supply chain coordination - between suppliers of a resource and buyers of resources.

As a host, the hREA language is one of the more intriguing applications I’ve seen emerge in the 5 years I’ve been following the project.

While it’ll still be a while before hREA might be leveraged in more “mainstream” commercial tools, the Cisco patent is somewhat encouraging.

thinking about the "moloch" problem...

and i'm starting to get the feeling that there is a intellectual disconnect between the:

foreground reality - visible failure of organizational coordination

background reality - invisible dynamics of the corporate competition mandate…

thinking about the "moloch" problem...

and i'm starting to get the feeling that there is a intellectual disconnect between the:

foreground reality - visible failure of organizational coordination

background reality - invisible dynamics of the corporate competition mandate

said another way: coordination "failure" isn't because of a lack of desire nor mechanisms to coordinate, not at all! 

people (and the organizations they create) want to coordinate! the thing is, under a capitalist and market-driven economy, they're not allowed! that's collusion - that's illegal. 

moloch isn't the result of social patterning, it's a result of corporate governance law.

relatedly: i also feel this is a significant reason why corporate adoption of blockchain failed. competitive businesses have a market expectation to withhold data and proprietary information. to assume corporations would willingly embrace blockchain for transparency and accountability mechanisms is a failure to recognize the existence of information rights.

Using @AnthropicAI Claude-Opus-200K to provide an overview the German book "Global Collaboration, Local Production" to English....

🧵

Introduction:  
- Recent global crises have highlighted the need to reorganize globalized value creation and production systems towards more sustainable processes.
- The Fab City approach envisions global collaboration in the digital sphere from ideation to product development using open source technologies, while physical production happens locally in a distributed manner.
- Fab Cities can contribute to the UN Sustainable Development Goals in areas like quality education, decent work and economic growth, infrastructure and innovation, sustainable cities, responsible consumption and production, and partnerships.

Part I: Frameworks and Theoretical Concepts
- The Fab City Full Stack provides a multiscalar operational framework to transform cities into self-sufficient, locally productive ecosystems.
- A Fab City can be understood as an alternative model for urban functioning, organizing a city as a cosmo-local space for production and consumption.
- Local production requires a holistic understanding to fully unleash its sustainability potential across social, economic and environmental dimensions.

Part II: Governance, Economics, and Enabling Technologies  
- Existing economic models are not suited for globally distributed open source development. Alternative approaches like "Creative Flows" are proposed.
- The transition to a Fab City can be supported by governance approaches like Transition Management.
- Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs) are explored as an alternative, decentralized governance model for Fab Cities.
- A Digital Product Passport for open source hardware and an index to measure a Fab City's circular economy status are presented as enabling technologies.
- Intellectual property rights and liability risks in the open source hardware context of Fab Cities are analyzed.

Part III: Distributed Innovation, Design, and Product Development
 - Citizen innovation formats should incorporate collaborative elements to attract altruistically motivated participants and increase submission quality.
- Adoption barriers and incentive systems for Fab Lab products are explored.
- The open source software ecosystem needed for open source hardware development is analyzed.
- New design approaches like distributed circular design are required for Fab Cities' distributed value creation.
- Co-creative product development enables local, cross-company production.

Part IV: Local Production 
- Makerspaces play a key role in facilitating the development and diffusion of open source hardware as central infrastructure and intermediaries.
- Usability of open source machine tools is crucial for their adoption but a non-trivial design challenge.
- The Open Lab Starter Kit aims to enable global diffusion of open source fabrication technology via Fab Labs.
- Open microfactories and networks of small local companies are presented as models to strengthen local production in urban environments.

In summary, the book provides interdisciplinary research insights into the multifaceted Fab City concept as a basis for designing value creation oriented towards local production and global collaboration. It invites further interdisciplinary research into the various aspects involved.

despite holding and staking $regen (gen1) for years - I am still not clear on its underlying token economics. Candidly, I don’t think there is was much token engineering applied to the $regen economics, which is why it went from $4 to $.05 in our trust - despite the fundamentally significant technical innovation that exists within the @regen_network core. 

The innovation behind the $regen (gen1) tech is still there - financial instruments supporting soil carbon sequestration and biodiversity, facilitating the distributed registration and indexing of on-the-ground landscape stewardship projects. that tech from my perspective - is priceless. The value of their work is what you spoke to in your first tweet. The technology is what they’re known and recognized for. The token was a funding mechanism.

The value I see in the tech is which is why I continue to hold, stake, and add to the $regen position in our trust. The collapse of the token price is unfortunate, but it’s not impossible for it to recover.

Price is a poor signal of value and markets can be wrong.

wrt tokens - I am personally resolved as to why tokens matter. I borrow from the ideas of @metacurrency + Ethan Roland and @gregory_landua in the plural forms of value (capital). There are multiple forms of value expressed in multiple currencies.

I see specific values in both $regen gen1 and $regen gen2, and I see mutual benefits in a possible relationship between the two. Where one is weak, the other has strengths. 

My take - a token is an instrument, a unit measurement representing participation in value flows. 

the $regen (gen1) value proposition is primarily intellectual, social, cultural,  created through a history of financial investments. Practically, this is their fundamental tech R&D, established relationships with partners, developers, node hosts, etc, and importantly - land stewards. Between RND, inc and @RegenFdn there is a rich ecosystem of stakeholders and fundamental innovations that I continue to have faith in and find myself often inspired by, both virtually and in person. 

the $regen (gen2) value proposition is primarily social, cultural, and experiential. The token allocation takes into account the historical  (multi-year) funding of public goods across @gitcoin and @Giveth - which, in practice,  means using blockchain to fund :

- open source tech, 
- identity and privacy tools,
- innovative climate initiatives, 
- civic technology, 
- land stewardship, 
- mutual aid,
- crisis relief,

and other benefit-oriented (and charity adjacent) work. $regen (gen2) is allocated to wallets who have long histories of funding these public goods/services using Ethereum as a means of economic coordination and transparency.

Our economic systems are failing to serve the interests of our living planet and the great majority of those who inhabit it. 

It is not our intention to restate the multiple crises that this mismatch has fuelled and continues to perpetuate. Instead, we are sharing our emerging thinking as we strive to build pathways towards alternative ways of organising society.

We are proposing Life-Ennobling Economics as a propositional vision and as a call to action. It is an ennobling invitation to break free from ideological constraints, to embrace the radical potential of emergent technologies, and to challenge the structural codes of our current socio-economic systems. Implicit in this opening position is an understanding that the structure and values of the economy must be in service to all forms of life (present, future, human, non-human and machine) providing an inclusive scaffold of care and respect.

3. Alignment with standards like ISO 20022
4. Easy to use APIs for developers inspired by UK/EU open banking
5. Intuitive UX for end users
6. Partnerships to drive adoption

The “Cottage Core” Economy

The cottage core economy is a new way of organizing a local economy within a neighborhood or village. It focuses on people working together to manage and share resources like food, water, and other essentials. The main goal is to create a more self-sufficient and resilient community that can better handle challenges and changes.

Key Principles:
The cottage core economy is based on the ideas of Elinor Ostrom, a Nobel Prize-winning economist who studied how communities can successfully manage shared resources. Her principles include:

1. Clear boundaries:
   - The cottage core economy works within a specific area, like a neighborhood or village, with clear boundaries.
   - Only people living within these boundaries can participate, which helps create a strong sense of community and shared responsibility.

2. Rules that fit local conditions:
   - The rules for sharing and exchanging resources are tailored to the specific needs and conditions of the local community.
   - Decisions about resources consider the unique characteristics, culture, and values of the neighborhood.

3. Community participation in decision-making:
   - The cottage core economy uses a decentralized approach, allowing all participants to have a say in making decisions and changing rules.
   - This can happen through regular community meetings, online forums, or voting systems, ensuring that every resident has a voice.

4. Monitoring:
   - The community elects "protectors" to monitor behavior and make sure everyone follows the rules.
   - Protectors oversee how resources are used and keep track of transactions within the network.

5. Graduated consequences:
   - "Enforcers" are elected to give appropriate consequences to participants who break the rules.
   - Enforcers work with protectors to identify and address violations. Repeated or serious violations may lead to temporary or permanent removal from the network.

6. Conflict resolution:
   - The community elects "trustees" to serve as neutral mediators when conflicts arise.
   - Trustees help resolve disputes among participants through mediation, arbitration, or community-based resolution processes.

7. Recognition by local authorities:
   - The cottage core economy works with the recognition and support of local authorities, ensuring that its activities align with the broader legal and institutional framework.
   - The community's right to self-organize and manage its resources is acknowledged and respected by external entities.

8. Coordination with larger systems:
   - The cottage core economy is designed to work with existing local systems, such as farmers' markets and supply chains.
   - This coordination allows for efficient resource management and decision-making at different levels, from individual households to the broader community and beyond.

Examples of Application:
1. Community Gardens: Residents can share information about their garden harvests, tools, and skills through the cottage core economy network. This helps identify surplus produce and enables the community to efficiently distribute food resources.

2. Tool Libraries: The network can facilitate the creation of a neighborhood tool library, where residents can borrow and lend tools, reducing the need for individual ownership and promoting sharing.

3. Skill-Sharing: Participants can offer their skills and services within the network, such as gardening advice, home repairs, or cooking classes, fostering a sense of community and interdependence.

By applying these principles and examples, the cottage core economy creates a framework for sustainable and equitable resource management at the local level. It empowers communities to take control of their resources, fosters shared responsibility, and promotes long-term resilience in the face of economic and environmental challenges.

The Cottage Core Economy relies on a simple yet effective integrated logic and data model that can be easily managed using a generic spreadsheet system. This approach allows for open access and updates through a cloud-based API, ensuring transparency and ease of use for all participants.

The data model consists of several interconnected spreadsheets that capture essential information about the community's resources, participants, and transactions. These spreadsheets include:

1. Participant Directory:
   - Contains a list of all registered participants in the Cottage Core Economy network.
   - Includes basic information such as name, address, contact details, and roles (e.g., protector, enforcer, trustee).
   - Helps maintain a clear record of who is involved in the network and their respective responsibilities.

2. Resource Inventory:
   - Tracks the available resources within the community, such as food, water, tools, skills, and services.
   - Includes details about the resource type, quantity, quality, and location.
   - Allows participants to easily identify and locate available resources when needed.

3. Transaction Log:
   - Records all transactions and exchanges that occur within the Cottage Core Economy network.
   - Includes information about the parties involved, the resources exchanged, quantities, and timestamps.
   - Provides a transparent and auditable trail of all economic activities within the community.

4. Community Rules and Agreements:
   - Documents the collectively agreed-upon rules, guidelines, and principles that govern the Cottage Core Economy.
   - Includes information on resource sharing, decision-making processes, conflict resolution mechanisms, and consequences for rule violations.
   - Ensures that all participants have access to and understand the community's operating framework.

5. Feedback and Dispute Records:
   - Captures feedback, complaints, and dispute cases submitted by participants.
   - Includes details about the parties involved, the nature of the issue, and the resolution process.
   - Helps trustees and the community track and resolve conflicts in a transparent and efficient manner.

The logic model defines how these spreadsheets interact and support the functioning of the Cottage Core Economy. For example:

- When a new participant joins the network, their information is added to the Participant Directory.
- As participants contribute or request resources, the Resource Inventory is updated accordingly.
- Transactions between participants are recorded in the Transaction Log, which references the Participant Directory and Resource Inventory.
- Community Rules and Agreements are consulted to ensure that all activities align with the collectively established guidelines.
- Feedback and disputes are logged in the Feedback and Dispute Records, triggering appropriate actions from trustees and the community.

By using a cloud-based API, the spreadsheet system allows for real-time updates and access to information for all participants. This ensures transparency, promotes trust, and enables efficient coordination within the Cottage Core Economy network.

Participants can easily contribute data, view relevant information, and engage in decision-making processes through a user-friendly interface, without requiring advanced technical skills. The API also enables integration with other local systems and databases, facilitating seamless coordination with existing resources and networks.

The integrated logic and data model for the Cottage Core Economy, built upon a generic spreadsheet system and a cloud-based API, provides a simple, transparent, and accessible framework for managing the community's resources and economic activities. This approach empowers all participants to actively engage in the network, fosters trust and collaboration, and supports the long-term resilience of the local economy.

People & Groups

People & Groups

The Cottage Core Economy is a local economic network that brings together a diverse range of people, groups, and organizations who all play important roles in shaping the community's success and well-being. These participants include:

1. Residents:
   - The heart of the Cottage Core Economy are the people and families living within the community's boundaries.
   - They actively contribute and use shared resources, working together to build a strong and sustainable local economy.
   - Residents have a deep personal investment in the success of the Cottage Core Economy, as it directly impacts their daily lives and future.

2. Local Businesses:
   - Small-scale enterprises, such as local farms, artisans, and service providers, are vital contributors to the Cottage Core Economy.
   - They supply essential goods and services to the community and benefit from the increased local economic activity generated by the network.
   - Local businesses are integral partners in creating a thriving and resilient local market.

3. Community Organizations:
   - Non-profit organizations, clubs, and associations operating within the community bring valuable resources, skills, and expertise to the Cottage Core Economy.
   - They play a key role in promoting social cohesion, well-being, and sustainability within the local area.
   - Community organizations are invested in the network's capacity to create positive change and foster a sense of belonging.

4. Local Government:
   - Elected officials, council members, and administrative staff are responsible for governing the community and ensuring its overall well-being.
   - They work to recognize, support, and integrate the Cottage Core Economy within the broader legal and institutional framework.
   - Local government has a vested interest in the network's ability to promote economic resilience, social welfare, and environmental sustainability.

5. Community Stewards:
   - Residents elected by the community to fulfill specific roles, such as mediators, monitors, and rule enforcers, are essential stewards of the Cottage Core Economy.
   - They work to uphold the integrity, fairness, and effectiveness of the network's governance mechanisms.
   - These stewards are entrusted with ensuring that the Cottage Core Economy operates smoothly and benefits all participants.

6. Neighboring Communities:
   - Adjacent neighborhoods, villages, or towns that interact with or are influenced by the Cottage Core Economy are important partners in regional resilience.
   - They engage in resource sharing, knowledge exchange, and collaboration with the local network.
   - Neighboring communities have a shared interest in the Cottage Core Economy's potential to create positive ripple effects across the region.

7. Researchers and Educators:
   - Scholars, students, and institutions interested in studying the Cottage Core Economy contribute valuable knowledge and insights to help refine and improve the network.
   - They analyze data, share findings, and provide recommendations to support the ongoing development of the local economic model.
   - Researchers and educators are committed to advancing understanding of innovative approaches like the Cottage Core Economy and sharing lessons learned with other communities.

8. Supporters and Allies:
   - Individuals, organizations, or institutions that provide financial, material, or technical support are crucial allies in the growth and success of the Cottage Core Economy.
   - They can include government agencies, private foundations, crowdfunding platforms, or individual donors who believe in the network's mission and potential.
   - Supporters and allies are invested in the Cottage Core Economy's ability to generate positive social, economic, and environmental outcomes.

Cottage Core Governance Structures & Roles

In the Cottage Core Economy, several key governance roles work together to ensure the smooth functioning, fairness, and sustainability of the local economic network. These roles are inspired by the rich history of commons management and draw upon the principles of shared responsibility, community empowerment, and stewardship. Let's explore each of these roles in more detail:

1. Councils:
   - Councils are the primary decision-making bodies within the Cottage Core Economy, representing the interests of all participants.
   - They are composed of elected representatives from the community, who serve for a specified term and are responsible for setting policies, guidelines, and rules for the network.
   - Councils hold regular meetings, open to all community members, to discuss important issues, propose changes, and vote on decisions that affect the entire network.
   - They strive to make decisions through consensus whenever possible, ensuring that all voices are heard and considered.

2. Circles:
   - Circles are smaller, focused groups within the Cottage Core Economy that address specific areas of concern or interest.
   - They can be formed around topics such as resource management, skill-sharing, conflict resolution, or community well-being.
   - Circles are open to all interested participants and provide a space for in-depth discussion, problem-solving, and collaboration.
   - They can make recommendations to the Councils and help implement decisions related to their area of focus.
   - Circles foster a sense of shared ownership and responsibility among participants, encouraging active engagement and contribution to the network.

3. Trustees:
   - Trustees are elected by the community to serve as impartial mediators and facilitators, helping to resolve conflicts and maintain the integrity of the Cottage Core Economy.
   - They are responsible for overseeing the fair and equitable distribution of resources, ensuring that everyone has access to what they need while preventing overuse or exploitation.
   - Trustees also act as guardians of the community's values, principles, and long-term well-being, making sure that decisions align with these core tenets.
   - They work closely with Councils and Circles to provide guidance, support, and oversight, fostering a culture of trust, transparency, and accountability.

4. Protectors:
   - Protectors are community members who volunteer to monitor the Cottage Core Economy's activities and ensure compliance with established rules and guidelines.
   - They serve as the eyes and ears of the network, identifying potential issues or violations and reporting them to the appropriate Circles or Councils.
   - Protectors also play an educational role, helping participants understand the rules and promoting a culture of respect, responsibility, and cooperation.
   - They work in collaboration with Trustees and Enforcers to maintain the integrity and fairness of the network.

5. Enforcers:
   - Enforcers are responsible for addressing rule violations and issuing appropriate consequences, as determined by the Councils and Circles.
   - They work to maintain the trust and stability of the Cottage Core Economy by ensuring that all participants are held accountable for their actions.
   - Enforcers follow a graduated approach to consequences, starting with gentle reminders and escalating to more serious measures only when necessary.
   - They strive to be fair, impartial, and transparent in their actions, communicating clearly with all parties involved and seeking to resolve issues in a manner that upholds the community's values.

These governance roles are designed to be flexible, adaptable, and responsive to the changing needs of the community. They are not meant to concentrate power in the hands of a few, but rather to distribute responsibility and decision-making authority among all participants in a fair and equitable manner.

Cottage Core Governance Structures & Roles

In the Cottage Core Economy, several key governance roles work together to ensure the smooth functioning, fairness, and sustainability of the local economic network. These roles are inspired by the rich history of commons management and… https://t.co/4PmSJU3i5q

Essentials of the Cottage Core - Goods & Services

The Cottage Core Economy revolves around the production, sale, and exchange of essential goods and services within a local community, empowering households, neighbors, and families to actively participate in a resilient and… https://t.co/2gI90oPMiu

Essentials of the Cottage Core - Goods & Services

The Cottage Core Economy revolves around the production, sale, and exchange of essential goods and services within a local community, empowering households, neighbors, and families to actively participate in a resilient and sustainable economic model. Drawing inspiration from cottage food laws, the Cottage Core Economy framework defines a range of homemade goods and services that can be safely produced and exchanged by community members.

Essentials of the Cottage Core Economy:

1. Home-Produced Foods:
   - Baked goods, jams, jellies, and preserves
   - Dried fruits, herbs, and spices
   - Pickled fruits and vegetables
   - Whole, uncut fresh fruits and vegetables
   - Nuts, seeds, and grains
   - Honey and syrups

2. Handcrafted Goods:
   - Pottery, jewelry, and textiles
   - Soaps and candles
   - Cut flowers and plants
   - Paper goods and journals
   - Upcycled or repurposed items

3. Community Services:
   - Cooking classes and workshops
   - Food delivery and catering
   - Farm and garden tours
   - Skill-sharing and educational events

Participation in the Cottage Core Economy:

1. Households:
   - Produce eligible foods and handcrafted goods from home kitchens and workshops
   - Sell products directly to neighbors and community members
   - Purchase essentials from other local producers
   - Attend workshops and classes to learn new skills

2. Neighbors:
   - Buy locally produced goods and services from within the community
   - Share resources, such as tools, ingredients, and knowledge
   - Collaborate on community projects and events
   - Provide support and encouragement to fellow producers

3. Families:
   - Involve all family members in the production and sale of cottage core goods
   - Pass down traditional recipes, techniques, and crafts to younger generations
   - Foster a sense of pride and connection to the local community
   - Participate in family-friendly workshops, tours, and educational events

By actively engaging in the Cottage Core Economy, households, neighbors, and families can contribute to the creation of a vibrant, self-sustaining local economy that values craftsmanship, sustainability, and community resilience. This participation not only provides a source of income for producers but also strengthens social bonds, promotes skill-sharing, and enhances the overall well-being of the community.

As the Cottage Core Economy evolves, participants can work together to establish guidelines, quality standards, and support networks that ensure the safety, fairness, and long-term success of the economic model. By prioritizing local production, direct exchange, and community empowerment, the Cottage Core Economy offers a path towards a more resilient, sustainable, and fulfilling future for all.

Basic Mechanism of Exchange - Local and Regional Currencies

Imagine a new way of exchanging goods and services within your local community – one that doesn't rely solely on traditional money. This is where a mutual credit community currency comes in! It's a special type of…

Basic Mechanism of Exchange - Local and Regional Currencies

Imagine a new way of exchanging goods and services within your local community – one that doesn't rely solely on traditional money. This is where a mutual credit community currency comes in! It's a special type of currency that works alongside regular money to help people trade with each other more easily and support their local economy.

What is a Mutual Credit Community Currency?

A mutual credit community currency is a way for people in a community to exchange goods and services without always using traditional money like dollars or euros. It's created and managed by the members of the community themselves, rather than by a bank or government.

Here's how it works:

1. When someone provides a good or service to another person in the community, they earn credit in the mutual credit system.

2. They can then use this credit to receive goods or services from someone else in the community.

3. The amount of credit someone can earn or use is based on trust and their contributions to the community.

4. The total amount of credit in the system always balances out – there's no overall profit or loss.

Benefits for Your Community:

Using a mutual credit community currency within your local Cottage Core Economy can have many benefits:

1. It encourages people to buy and sell goods and services within the community, which helps support local businesses and keeps money circulating locally.

2. It provides a backup system during times of economic uncertainty, helping to keep the local economy stable.

3. It allows people to access credit based on their reputation and contributions to the community, without having to pay interest.

4. It strengthens community bonds by fostering trust, cooperation, and a sense of mutual support among members.

5. It can even help connect your community with neighboring communities that use similar systems, creating a larger network of exchange and collaboration.

Getting Started:

To start a mutual credit community currency in your Cottage Core Economy, here are some basic steps:

1. Get a group of interested community members together to discuss the idea and decide on the rules and goals of the system.

2. Determine how much credit each person can start with based on their skills, needs, and contributions to the community.

3. Choose a way to keep track of transactions, either using a physical ledger or a secure digital system.

4. Spread the word and encourage others in the community to participate.

5. As the system grows, reach out to nearby communities to explore the possibility of connecting your mutual credit networks.

By introducing a mutual credit community currency, your Cottage Core Economy can become even more vibrant, resilient, and interconnected. It's a say for the community to come together, support one another, and build a stronger, more sustainable local economy for everyone.

EXAMPLES

Sample Dialogue: Treasurer Using Compass (hREA Agent)

Treasurer: "Hello Compass, I need to…

Assuming natural language interface with agent driven and validated hREA actions - it could be possible to more efficiently operate, govern, and coordinate cooperatives. 

EXAMPLES

Sample Dialogue: Treasurer Using Compass (hREA Agent)

Treasurer: "Hello Compass, I need to prepare a budget report for our upcoming general assembly."

Compass: "Welcome, Treasurer. I'm here to help you prepare a transparent budget report for the general assembly. Shall we start with an overview of our current financial status?"

Treasurer: "Yes, please provide a summary of our current financial position."

Compass: "Based on our hREA data, here's our financial summary:

- Common Resource Pool: $250,000
- Monthly Operating Costs: $45,000
- Projected Member Contributions (next month): $70,000
- Pending Member Economic Participation: $15,000

This information is accessible to all members. Would you like to explore any category in more detail?"

Treasurer: "That's a good start. Now, let's discuss the proposed community garden project."

Compass: "The community garden project was approved by member vote last month with a $30,000 budget estimate. It aligns with our sustainable development goals. How would you like to proceed with resource allocation?"

Treasurer: "Let's allocate $25,000 to start. How will this impact our overall resource distribution?"

Compass: "I'll create an economic event for this allocation. Here's the impact:

- Current Common Resource Pool: $250,000
- Proposed Allocation to Community Garden: $25,000
- Remaining in Common Resource Pool: $225,000

This 10% allocation requires ratification at the general assembly, per our bylaws. Shall I add this to the assembly agenda?"

Treasurer: "Yes, please add it to the agenda for ratification."

Compass: "Added to the agenda. I've prepared a brief outlining the project's goals, community benefits, and financial implications. Should I share this with all members before the assembly?"

Treasurer: "Yes, please share it with everyone."

Compass: "I've shared the brief through our secure communication channel. Now, would you like a projection of our finances for the next quarter to aid in long-term planning?"

Treasurer: "Yes, please provide a three-month projection."

Compass: "Here's a three-month projection based on historical data, member feedback, and the proposed garden project:

Month 1:
- Member Contributions: $70,000
- Operational Costs: $70,000 ($45,000 regular + $25,000 garden project)
- Net Change in Common Pool: $0

Month 2:
- Member Contributions: $72,000 (3% increase)
- Operational Costs: $46,000 (2% increase)
- Net Addition to Common Pool: +$26,000

Month 3:
- Member Contributions: $74,000 (3% growth)
- Operational Costs: $47,000 (2% increase)
- Net Addition to Common Pool: +$27,000

Cumulative 3-month change: +$53,000

Shall I generate a detailed report with these projections and an alternative scenario where the garden project is delayed by a month?"

Treasurer: "Yes, please generate that report."

Compass: "I've created the report including:
1. Current financial status
2. Proposed garden project allocation
3. Three-month projection with immediate project start
4. Alternative scenario with one-month delay
5. Implications for our goals and member benefits

It's available on our secure document platform. Should I notify members to review it before the assembly?"

Treasurer: "Yes, please notify everyone and encourage pre-assembly review."

Compass: "Notification sent. Members are encouraged to review and submit questions or comments through our participatory decision-making platform. This should facilitate more efficient discussions at the assembly."

I’ve had a recent influx of follows recently. 

To re-introduce myself…

👋🏻 Hi, I’m Todd Youngblood - based in Denver. 

I’m interested in technology and policy in service to humanity, our living planet, and creative expression. 

You’ll likely see content from me about decentralized or peer-to-peer technologies, civic innovation, small or local places-based economics, insights or policy on new structures of economic organizing, banking or trust systems and related tools and technologies, counter-corporate ideas, some memes, and possibly some sh*tposts.

I consider myself a “counter-corporate MBA” challenging the notion that shareholder corporations are the best way to provision the goods and services that are most essential to human and non-human needs. 

Ultimately, I care most about family, friends, and the shared life and spaces we hold - a “common house” - or economy as it is also known. 

The question that most often guides me is “how can we design and create tools and technologies to best serve the health and wellbeing of Planet Earth and the common house. 

I’m quite jazzed and inspired by alternative forms of organizing as structures/mediums for collective self expression - the commons, co-ops, for-benefit organizations, purpose trusts, and more. If any of these are also interesting to you, please reach out. I open my calendar for lively discussion. 

Professionally, I’m a consultant in information and graphic design and data engineering, specifically focused on prototype and concept development for web and mobile applications. Maybe you’ve seen some of my prototypes like https://t.co/52INs5BCDt or https://t.co/MThrAH1elc. 

I’m available for hire through a consulting co-op called https://t.co/Xb19M30Wn9 - DM if interested.

Great to meet you!

P.S. As an American, I apologize to other global citizens about the theater that is U.S. politics on the global stage and its prevalence in non-U.S. social media. I survive with generous use of word muting.

CMV: The failure of modern fintech and banking structures is a critical disservice and should be met with nothing less than class action lawsuits where appropriate.

There is a non-zero relationship between the failures our economic institutions and financial infrastructure (including their regulated agreements) and critical issues like homelessness.

Yes - this includes The Fed, incumbent banks, and modern fintech. The entire system is interconnected.

To make any meaningful progress, we need to think carefully about the design and engineering of not just our public accounting infrastructure, but the very social agreements underpinning modern finance and banking at the state and federal level.

I would argue that it's challenging for the financial services sector to fully play its role as critical infrastructure[1], essential to any economic livelihood, while perpetuating the existing model that was reinforced by the 2008 government bailouts.

Corporate socialism, via internalization of profit and externalization of risk, is arguably one of the most problematic forms of economic policy. It raises serious ethical concerns and contributes to economic inequality.

At this point - functionally - the ideal of free market capitalism in the U.S. financial sector is significantly compromised. How can there be truly free markets if the medium of exchange (fiat currency) and the intermediary of exchange (banks) appear to operate outside of normal market dynamics?

The paradigm of banking that emerged post-2008 could be described as a form of 'zombie banking' via bailouts. Incumbents, potentially less sensitive to free market competition, might reasonably expect government intervention in future crises - after all, there's now a precedent.

Building a healthy and resilient economy will likely require fundamental changes to these systems and agreements. However, I see few actors or organizations substantially addressing or even discussing the root problem.

This is a complex issue that deserves more public discourse and critical examination.

[1] https://t.co/ufrG07CzH7

COMMON SENSE REGULATION FOR DECENTRALIZED FINANCE

*saturday nights notes to the digital commons*

While digital assets and smart contracts (broadly known as "decentralized finance" [DeFi] or "crypto") have waited over a decade for regulatory clarity from the SEC, banking regulators, and federal banking infrastructure (e.g., @custodiabank), it's time we shift our focus to building alliances from the ground up.

If there's one topic I wish would be covered in the presidential debates, it's DeFi.

It's high time we redirect our energy into local alliance building between state and federal institutions. Our focus should be on developing a common-sense regulatory framework and establishing regulatory sandboxes (similar to the UK model) that bridge civic and state bodies.

My hope is that decentralized finance can find new partners in both state and federal governments. These partnerships should apply blockchain technology in meaningful ways, such as public goods funding and participatory budgeting, that truly embody the benefits of blockchain and distributed ledgers. This approach stands in stark contrast to the fraud and speculative excesses currently running rampant in the unregulated crypto scene.

Regulatory clarity emerges through demonstrated beneficial applications of the technology. This, I believe, requires a controlled sandbox environment (see the UK's Financial Conduct Authority sandbox as an example).

The U.S. Postal Service (@USPS) could be an ideal partner for a regulatory sandbox. Their concept of "digital stamps" closely aligns with the potential for local basket currencies.

Imagine the Department of Agriculture (@USDA) collaborating across counties with regional agricultural cooperatives, leveraging local or regional currencies that civic bodies use at farmers markets or county fairs.

This vision will become possible when "crypto" matures beyond its current state of brazen pyramid schemes.

The first presidential candidate to outline a minimal, common-sense framework for the regulation of digital assets and smart contracts could probably win the race.

"I'm afraid we will continue to buy pieces of hardware and then put programs on them - when what we should be doing is looking at the underlying thing, which is the total flow of information to any organization, activity, company, or what have you. ABSTRACT 

Permacomputing is a nascent concept and a community of practice centred around design principles that embrace limits and constraints as a positive thing in computational culture, and on creativity with scarce computational resources. As a result, permacomputing aims to provide a countervoice to digital practices that promote maximisation, hyper-consumption and waste. It seeks to encourage practices as an applied critique of contemporary computer technology that privileges maximalist aesthetics where more pixels, more frame rate, more computation and more power equals more potential at any cost and without any consequences. We believe that such a critical practice can be relevant to artists, designers and cultural practitioners working with computer and network technology who are interested in engaging with environmental issues. This is particularly relevant given the tendency in art, design and cultural production to rely on tools and techniques designed to maximise productivity and mass consumption. In this paper, we argue for the potential of permacomputing as a rich framework for exploring creative design constraints building on a long history of applying constraints in art, design and cultural practices. Because of the need to reconfigure the modes of production and organisation within computational practices, this calls for a different understanding of aesthetics, one that goes beyond the formal evaluation of how things look, but addresses how aesthetics can also be systems of relations, sensing and making sense that are already present in the process of making. We will also discuss the challenges faced by permacomputing practitioners, such as the complicated link with retro-computing, post-digital culture and nostalgia, as well as the problem of constraints in relation to the aesthetisation of poverty, and more generally what it means to work with self-imposed limits in a more privileged socioeconomic context.

How about cyclical debt forgiveness for managing economic inequity?

The debt jubilee (Yovel in Hebrew) system, first codified around 1200 BCE in Levitical law but likely building on earlier Mesopotamian clean slate traditions dating to 2400 BCE, operated as a sophisticated economic reset mechanism with two primary cycles - the seven-year Shmita (release) and the 49/50-year Jubilee, with the latter implementing full debt forgiveness and property reversion using a formula where asset values depreciated proportionally to the years remaining until the next Jubilee (Value = Base * Years_Remaining/50). The system distinguished between forgivable debts (personal, agricultural) and exempt obligations (commercial, foreign), while incorporating anti-avoidance measures such as mandatory lending requirements and interest prohibitions between citizens, alongside social safety mechanisms like the Prozbul exception for critical debts; this created a balanced approach that prevented permanent wealth concentration while maintaining property rights and family economic units through a combination of inalienable land ownership, cyclical redistribution, and clearly defined jurisdictional constraints between urban and rural regions - a system that remained influential through the First Temple period (c. 960-586 BCE) and has continued to inspire economic thought through modern times.

There's something magical about how we've woven the world together. In just a few decades, humanity has created an incredible tapestry of connections – fiber optic threads spanning oceans, radio waves dancing through the air, and satellites gracefully orbiting above. This achievement represents one of the most beautiful expressions of human cooperation and creativity in our history. 

The following is a hopeful critique of Social Media by a practitioner and a theoretician.

The Promise We Glimpse

The Promise We Glimpse

Every day, we see glimpses of what this wonderful technology can do:
- Grandparents sharing precious moments with far-away grandchildren
- Students in remote villages accessing the world's knowledge
- Artists finding their first audiences and lifelong collaborators
- Scientists working across borders to solve global challenges
- Communities coming together to support members in need

These moments remind us of the profound human desire to connect, share, and create together. They show us that at our core, we long to use technology in ways that lift each other up and bring out our best selves.

Where We've Drifted

Yet something has pulled us off course. The platforms we use most often have gradually shifted their focus from nurturing these meaningful connections to harvesting our attention. It's not that anyone set out to create this situation – it emerged from a business model that seemed innocent enough at first: advertising-supported social networks.

But like a garden overtaken by a single aggressive species, this model has crowded out other possibilities. Our screens have become less like windows connecting us to each other and more like mirrors reflecting back our own impulses, anxieties, and biases. The algorithms, designed to maximize engagement, have learned to feed us not what nourishes our spirits but what keeps us scrolling.

The Hidden Costs

This shift has taken a toll on our collective wellbeing:
- Genuine conversations get lost in streams of quick reactions
- Deep thinking struggles to compete with viral content
- Creative energy gets scattered across endless feeds
- Real human connection often…

The Hidden Costs

This shift has taken a toll on our collective wellbeing:
- Genuine conversations get lost in streams of quick reactions
- Deep thinking struggles to compete with viral content
- Creative energy gets scattered across endless feeds
- Real human connection often feels more distant, not closer
- Our precious attention becomes fragmented and depleted

It's as if we've built a magnificent concert hall but filled it with slot machines instead of musicians.

The Better Path Forward

Here's the wonderful news: we can choose a different path. All the technical brilliance that built these platforms can be redirected toward more wholesome ends. We already have everything we need to create digital spaces that:

1. Celebrate depth and nuance
2. Nurture creativity and collaboration
3. Strengthen authentic connections
4. Honor our attention as sacred
5. Protect our privacy and dignity

Seeds of Change

Look closely and you'll find these better alternatives already growing:
- Community-owned platforms where members set the priorities
- Digital cooperatives that share resources and knowledge
- Creative commons where artists and technologists collaborate
- Learning networks that prioritize understanding over metrics
- Local digital spaces that strengthen neighborhood bonds

These initiatives show us that another way is possible – one that aligns with our highest values and aspirations.

A Call to Action

The Heart of the Matter

A Call to Action

We don't need to tear everything down and start over. Instead, we can begin by nurturing these healthier alternatives while gradually withdrawing our energy from spaces that don't serve our collective wellbeing. Like tending a garden, this work requires patience, care, and steady attention.

Here's how we might begin:
- Support platforms and tools that respect our humanity
- Create more spaces for meaningful dialogue
- Share our skills and knowledge generously
- Prioritize quality of connection over quantity
- Invest our time and resources in digital public spaces

The Heart of the Matter

The current advertising-centered model acts like a gravitational well, pulling our collective energy into patterns that often leave us feeling depleted and disconnected. But we can choose to direct our creative force toward something more life-giving – digital spaces that reflect the full spectrum of human potential.

Remember: every great human achievement began with people coming together to build something they believed in. We've already proven we can create technological wonders. Now let's ensure these wonders serve our highest aspirations and deepest needs for connection, creativity, and meaning.

The infrastructure we've built deserves to carry our hopes, not just our clicks. Our minds deserve spaces that help them flourish, not just engage. Our future deserves technologies that bring out the best in us, not just the most predictable.

Together, we can create digital spaces worthy of the human spirit. The code has been written. The connections have been made. All that remains is for us to choose, with gentle determination, to build something better.

Let's begin.

Modern theory (and law) of corporations put shareholder value as the principal outcome, where founders (at its origin) create the company to provide a good/service/platform. 

Over time, the business equation changes from value created through its core offering for customers, to value extracted through its core offering for shareholders.

text:
There lies a profound irony in how modern KYC requirements, in their attempt to formalize and standardize trust, have effectively stripped it of its most essential qualities. 

The very term "Know Your Customer" has become a misnomer, for what we have created is not a framework for genuine knowledge but rather a system of documentation and verification that serves as a proxy for actual understanding.

This trend will only continue as local and regional banks continue to merge into the great fiat Monolith and the ties to local value and culture are severed.
